nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chicken curry (medium-fat preparation) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chicken, broiler, meat only, cooked, stewed (NDB 05064) and Spices, curry powder (NDB 02010)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Chicken Jalfrezi is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry (medium-fat) as the primary anchor, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken breast and curry spice blend entries. The input record's per-100g values (energy 137 kcal, protein 10.9 g, fat 8.0 g, carbohydrate 5.7 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN chicken curry (medium-fat) range of 120–145 kcal, 10–12 g protein, 7–9 g fat, 4–7 g carbohydrate per 100 g and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 271 mg/100 g is elevated relative to home-cooked references (~150–180 mg) and reflects restaurant-style preparation. Potassium estimated at 280 mg/100 g from ingredient composition (chicken ~250 mg, tomato ~237 mg, bell pepper ~211 mg, onion ~146 mg weighted by proportion). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available in ICMR-NIN for this dish; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — chicken curry analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Jalfrezi. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous Indian chicken curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The dish is predominantly protein and fat with low carbohydrate (5.71 g/100 g), consistent with a low GI profile. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (35 × 20.0) / 100 = 7.0 (low GL). Carbohydrate per serving = 5.71 g/100 g × 350 g = 19.99 g ≈ 20 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 271 mg/100 g; restaurant-style preparation may use additional salt
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or chutneys; the dish's potassium content (~280 mg/100 g from bell peppers, tomatoes, and chicken) partially offsets sodium load, but a 350 g serving still delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, which represents 41% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.71 g/100 g), high protein (10.86 g/100 g), estimated GI 35
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Jalfrezi is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-glycaemic accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the low carbohydrate content, high protein, and anti-hyperglycaemic spices (turmeric, cinnamon via garam masala) support glycaemic control, and a 350 g serving contributes approximately 20 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of 7.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat low at 2.29 g/100 g; trans fat minimal at 0.06 g/100 g; anti-inflammatory spice profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish presents a favourable cardiovascular profile with low saturated fat (2.29 g/100 g), negligible trans fat (0.06 g/100 g), and anti-inflammatory ingredients including turmeric (curcumin), garlic, ginger, and tomato lycopene; patients should ensure cooking oil is a MUFA-rich option (olive or canola) and monitor sodium intake to avoid compounding hypertensive cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile; nightshade vegetables present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric, ginger, garlic, and garam masala provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit; however, bell peppers, tomatoes, and green chillies are nightshade vegetables and individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor their response, as solanine may aggravate symptoms in a subset of RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Jalfrezi (also spelled Jhalfrazie, Jalfrazi) is a popular Indian curry dish originating from the Bengal region of the Indian subcontinent and widely served across Indian restaurants in Singapore and Southeast Asia. The dish features tender pieces of chicken — typically boneless breast or thigh — marinated in yogurt and spices, then stir-fried and simmered in a vibrant, spicy, tangy tomato-based gravy with sautéed onions, bell peppers (capsicum), and green chillies. The name derives from the Bengali 'jhal' (spicy) and 'frezi' (stir-fry), reflecting its characteristic cooking technique that distinguishes it from slow-cooked curries. Key spices include turmeric, cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, and garlic, which together create a bold, aromatic flavour profile with moderate to high heat. The texture is semi-dry compared to butter chicken or korma, with visible vegetable pieces and a thick, clingy sauce. Nutritionally, Chicken Jalfrezi is a protein-rich, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish: per 100 g it provides approximately 137 kcal, 10.9 g protein, 8.0 g fat (2.3 g saturated), and 5.7 g carbohydrate with 1.4 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 271 mg/100 g in restaurant preparations. The dish has a low estimated glycaemic index (~35) and is considered suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis, provided sodium is moderated. It is halal and gluten-free. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 350 g (1 plate), providing around 480 kcal and 38 g protein.

confidence_reason: Chicken Jalfrezi is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry (medium-fat) as the closest approved primary source and are consistent with the input record's values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Potassium is estimated from ingredient composition. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of an exact approved-source match.

Sodium at 271 mg/100 g reflects restaurant-style preparation; home-cooked versions typically contain 150–180 mg/100 g. The input record listed the food name as 'Chicken Jhalfrazie' — canonical English spelling normalised to 'Chicken Jalfrezi'. The input source URL (foodo.sg) is a delivery/menu platform and is not an approved nutrient source; nutrient values have been re-anchored to ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central. Potassium 280 mg/100 g is an ingredient-composition estimate and should be replaced with a direct analytical value when available.
